message.content:
Work on Linear issue ENG-7339:

<issue identifier="ENG-7339">
<title>History of RA MRR in `view_mrr_by_customer_by_day`</title>
<description>
**Context**

See this: [https://stable.metabaseapp.com/question/16006-ra-revenue-snapshot](<https://stable.metabaseapp.com/question/16006-ra-revenue-snapshot>)

* We want historcal ARR for RA's, not just currents state of the world
* Should be able to recycle some logic from `churn_predication_base` table / `view_customer_cumulative_metrics`
* Final state should live in `view_mrr_by_customer_by_day`

**Acceptance Criteria**

* Share with Connor & Arushi
</description>
<team name="Engineering"/>
<project name="Launch registered agents"/>
<issue-relations><related><issue-ref identifier="ENG-7307" title="Metabase question: Median time to adding a RA"/></related></issue-relations>
</issue>

<comment-thread comment-id="7e0939f1-0f37-4dff-89da-3ab2b3aab54f">
<comment author="Adrian Torchiana" created-at="2026-02-18T17:26:51.537Z">
More context for the bot: we could add a new column to [https://github.com/useStable/redshift-sql/blob/eafaa470c605621aba4a83f45f70e1c97ee927d8/views/standard_data_refresh/01_mrr_history_from_stripe_sigma_subscription_item_change_events.sql#L4](<https://github.com/useStable/redshift-sql/blob/eafaa470c605621aba4a83f45f70e1c97ee927d8/views/standard_data_refresh/01_mrr_history_from_stripe_sigma_subscription_item_change_events.sql#L4>), `view_mrr_by_customer_by_day`, telling us the MRR from registered agents for a given customer on a given event_day

See how we are already using `mysql_prices` and `mysql_products` in that view definition.

Importantly, we'll have to make sure our RA MRR is calculated correctly **regardless of payment cadence**, i.e. make sure it is correct for people on annual billing as well as monthly billing (and our other rare payment cadence edge cases too, like semi-annual)
</comment>
</comment-thread>

<comment-thread comment-id="37c2ef90-2422-4fba-89ab-23efa69b490a"><comment author="Adrian Torchiana" created-at="2026-02-18T17:27:35.506Z">Reminder that a lot of the project metrics Registered Agent reporting could be built on top of our `view_mrr_by_customer_by_day` and/or `view_customer_cumulative_metrics` foundations, especially after this ticket is done</comment></comment-thread>

<comment-thread comment-id="4ab7ee51-545a-4dd5-8caf-17a2a3f464fd">
<comment author="Connor Harness" created-at="2026-02-20T05:24:13.501Z"><user id="b48db867-3445-4ab0-ac2c-47690f879e30">adrian</user> or <user id="52221374-e1c2-4075-9325-7f2cb9da6324">ben</user> does the mrr that stripe is reporting reflect non-discounted mrr?</comment>
<comment author="Adrian Torchiana" created-at="2026-02-20T16:02:42.497Z">
Correct, there's a setting in our Stripe that controls this

We reflect discounted MRR *only* if the discount is permanent. Otherwise MRR ignores discounts.
</comment>
<comment author="Adrian Torchiana" created-at="2026-02-20T16:03:54.885Z">![Screenshot 2026-02-20 at 8.03.36 AM.png](https://uploads.linear.app/bf89eb10-62b7-4fe4-a054-24721cd6de52/116523c0-3113-40e2-8d84-f1b3577f8635/6a07f62d-efa0-40ee-89a9-c38e730f274f?signature=eyJhbGciOiJIUzI1NiIsInR5cCI6IkpXVCJ9.eyJwYXRoIjoiL2JmODllYjEwLTYyYjctNGZlNC1hMDU0LTI0NzIxY2Q2ZGU1Mi8xMTY1MjNjMC0zMTEzLTQwZTItOGQ4NC1mMWIzNTc3Zjg2MzUvNmEwN2Y2MmQtZWZhMC00MGVlLTg5YTktYzM4ZTczMGYyNzRmIiwiaWF0IjoxNzcyNDYwMTY4LCJleHAiOjE3NzI1MDMzNjh9.MrQDkvJV-KXmybvvFmCnTg-1mv9XIxjkV69L5Du7VWo)</comment>
</comment-thread>

can you review this linear ticket and come up with a plan of how we would approach this?

content.text: Let me first examine the current MRR view structure:

content.content:
1→/*
     2→================================================================================
     3→TABLE: view_mrr_by_customer_by_day
     4→
     5→This is the preferred source of truth for customer MRR, ARR, and plan_type data.
     6→
     7→DATA SHAPE:
     8→- 1 observation per customer per day
     9→- Includes every day from a customer's first subscription event to the latest event date in the dataset
    10→- Forward-fills MRR values for days with no subscription changes (last observation carried forward)
    11→- Provides historical tracking of MRR changes over time
    12→
    13→KEY COLUMNS:
    14→- customer_id: Stripe customer ID
    15→- event_day: Date (one row per day per customer)
    16→- mrr: Monthly Recurring Revenue for this customer on this day
    17→- plan_type: Current base plan (launch, grow, scale, etc.)
    18→- plan_type_category: Categorization of plan_type as 'Sales Led' (custom, enterprise, platform) or 'Self-Serve' (all others)
    19→- payment_cadence: Billing frequency (month, year, quarter, etc.)
    20→- churned_mrr/expansion_mrr/new_mrr: MRR changes
    21→- mrr_transferred_in: MRR amount transferred INTO this plan_type from another plan type
    22→  Positive value on first day of new plan, captures previous day's MRR amount
    23→  Only counts when MRR remains positive (not churned)
    24→- mrr_transferred_out: MRR amount transferred OUT of this plan_type to another plan type
    25→  Negative value on last day of old plan, captures current day's MRR amount
    26→  Only counts when MRR remains positive on both sides
    27→  IMPORTANT: SUM(mrr_transferred_in) + SUM(mrr_transferred_out) = 0 across all time
    28→- cumulative_trial_days: Total trial days accumulated as of this event_day (forward-filled)
    29→  Many 2025+ plan_type 'grow' customers have ~90-93 days (3-month trial) on their first MRR day
    30→  Enables distinguishing truly new customers (0 days) from trial conversions
    31→- initial_plan_type: The customer's plan type on day 0 (constant for all rows)
    32→  Use this for NRR calculations grouped by plan type to avoid double-counting
    33→- initial_plan_type_for_nrr: Same as initial_plan_type, but adjusted for edge cases:
    34→  - If customer started on grow/scale but switched to custom within 14 days, this is 'custom'
    35→  - This handles sales-led deals where customers were temporarily on standard plans
    36→- day_number_for_nrr: Adjusted day_number for NRR calculations
    37→  - For most customers: equals day_number
    38→  - For "early custom switch" customers: shifted so first custom day = 0
    39→  - Example: customer starts on scale day 0, switches to custom day 1
    40→    -> day_number_for_nrr = -1 for day 0 (scale), 0 for day 1 (custom)
    41→  - This ensures NRR uses the CUSTOM MRR as the basis, not the temporary scale MRR
    42→  - Use day_number_for_nrr = 0 and = 365 for NRR calculations
    43→
    44→COMMON USAGE PATTERNS:
    45→
    46→1. Latest total ARR across all customers:
    47→   SELECT sum(mrr) * 12.0 as latest_arr
    48→   FROM view_mrr_by_customer_by_day
    49→   WHERE event_day = (SELECT MAX(event_day) FROM view_mrr_by_customer_by_day)
    50→
    51→2. ARR by plan type (current):
    52→   SELECT plan_type, SUM(mrr) * 12.0 AS arr
    53→   FROM view_mrr_by_customer_by_day
    54→   WHERE event_day = (SELECT MAX(event_day) FROM view_mrr_by_customer_by_day)
    55→   GROUP BY plan_type
    56→   ORDER BY arr DESC
    57→
    58→3. MRR trends over time:
    59→   SELECT event_day, SUM(mrr) as total_mrr
    60→   FROM view_mrr_by_customer_by_day
    61→   GROUP BY event_day
    62→   ORDER BY event_day
    63→
    64→4. Individual customer MRR history (this allows you to see their plan_type and MRR changes over time):
    65→   SELECT event_day, mrr, plan_type
    66→   FROM view_mrr_by_customer_by_day
    67→   WHERE customer_id = 'cus_...'
    68→   ORDER BY event_day
    69→
    70→5. MRR change analysis by month (churn, expansion, new which contribute to aggregate MRR growth):
    71→   SELECT
    72→     DATE_TRUNC('month', event_day) AS month,
    73→     SUM(expansion_mrr) AS sum_expansion_mrr,
    74→     SUM(new_mrr) AS sum_new_mrr,
    75→     SUM(churned_mrr) AS sum_churned_mrr
    76→   FROM view_mrr_by_customer_by_day
    77→   WHERE event_day >= '2024-01-01'
    78→   GROUP BY DATE_TRUNC('month', event_day)
    79→   ORDER BY month ASC
    80→
    81→6. Immutable ARR history by plan type (tracks transfers between plan types):
    82→   SELECT
    83→     plan_type,
    84→     DATE_TRUNC('month', event_day) AS month,
    85→     SUM(new_mrr) AS new_mrr,
    86→     SUM(expansion_mrr) AS expansion_mrr,
    87→     SUM(churned_mrr) AS churned_mrr,
    88→     SUM(mrr_transferred_in) AS mrr_transferred_in,
    89→     SUM(mrr_transferred_out) AS mrr_transferred_out
    90→   FROM view_mrr_by_customer_by_day
    91→   WHERE event_day >= '2024-01-01'
    92→   GROUP BY plan_type, DATE_TRUNC('month', event_day)
    93→   ORDER BY plan_type, month ASC
    94→
    95→7. 12-month NRR by initial plan type (CORRECT approach using day_number_for_nrr):
    96→   -- Uses day_number_for_nrr which adjusts for "early custom switch" edge cases
    97→   -- where customers were temporarily on grow/scale before their real custom deal
    98→   --
    99→   -- For the denominator, we use active_start_mrr_change when available (so day-0
   100→   -- expansion counts as expansion, not as part of the basis). We fall back to mrr
   101→   -- when active_start_mrr_change is 0 or NULL due to edge cases in the underlying data.
   102→   WITH first_nrr_dates AS (
   103→       SELECT event_day as first_nrr_date, customer_id
   104→       FROM view_mrr_by_customer_by_day
   105→       WHERE day_number_for_nrr = 0  -- Use adjusted day number
   106→   )
   107→   SELECT
   108→       initial_plan_type_for_nrr,
   109→       SUM(CASE WHEN day_number_for_nrr = 365 THEN mrr ELSE 0 END) /
   110→       NULLIF(SUM(CASE WHEN day_number_for_nrr = 0
   111→           THEN COALESCE(NULLIF(active_start_mrr_change, 0), mrr) ELSE 0 END), 0) as nrr_12_months
   112→   FROM view_mrr_by_customer_by_day v
   113→   JOIN first_nrr_dates fnd ON fnd.customer_id = v.customer_id
   114→   WHERE fnd.first_nrr_date < DATEADD(month, -13, CURRENT_DATE)
   115→     AND v.customer_churned_within_one_day IS FALSE
   116→     AND v.initial_plan_type_for_nrr IN ('grow', 'scale', 'custom')
   117→   GROUP BY initial_plan_type_for_nrr
   118→
   119→PERFORMANCE NOTES:
   120→- Queries source tables directly (no intermediate table copies)
   121→- Uses atomic swap pattern for zero-downtime refreshes
   122→- Recreated from scratch on each refresh
   123→
   124→DEPLOYMENT PATTERN:
   125→- Uses atomic swap wrapped in BEGIN/COMMIT transaction for true atomicity
   126→- Step 1: CREATE TABLE view_mrr_by_customer_by_day_new (builds new table)
   127→- Step 2: BEGIN; DROP old table; RENAME _new to current; COMMIT;
   128→- Ensures no partial state if swap fails
   129→
   130→IMPORTANT - PRODUCTION USAGE:
   131→This table is heavily used in production Metabase dashboards for MRR/ARR reporting.
   132→Changes to refresh schedule or data availability will impact business reporting.
   133→Always prefer view_mrr_by_customer_by_day for MRR/ARR reporting to ensure consistency and accuracy.
   134→
   135→================================================================================
   136→*/
   137→
   138→-- Step 1: Drop the _new table if it exists (cleanup from any previous failed runs)
   139→DROP TABLE IF EXISTS view_mrr_by_customer_by_day_new;
   140→
   141→-- Step 2: Create the new table with fresh data
   142→CREATE TABLE view_mrr_by_customer_by_day_new
   143→DISTKEY(customer_id)
   144→SORTKEY(event_day)
   145→AS
   146→WITH mrr_changes_by_day AS (
   147→    SELECT
   148→    DATE_TRUNC('day', TO_TIMESTAMP(subscription_change_events.event_timestamp, 'YYYY-MM-DD HH24:MI:SS.MS')) as event_day,
   149→    subscription_change_events.customer_id,
   150→    SUM(subscription_change_events.mrr_change) AS mrr_change_within_day
   151→    FROM public.stripe_data_pipeline_subscription_item_change_events AS subscription_change_events
   152→    GROUP BY
   153→    subscription_change_events.customer_id,
   154→    event_day
   155→),
   156→/*
   157→See comment below about customer_id = 'cus_I9lC1fzlIUqLxJ' and
   158→the edge case where a customer has multiple ACTIVE_START events on the same day
   159→This will be used to get ACTIVE_START MRR on a given day, excluding starts that are cancelled on the same day
   160→See the CASE WHEN active_end_products_by_day.canceled_product_id IS NULL logic in the active_start_mrr_changes_by_day CTE
   161→*/
   162→active_end_products_by_day AS (
   163→    SELECT
   164→    product_id AS canceled_product_id,
   165→    customer_id,
   166→    event_timestamp
   167→    FROM public.stripe_data_pipeline_subscription_item_change_events
   168→    WHERE event_type = 'ACTIVE_END'
   169→),
   170→/*
   171→Having the sum of ACTIVE_START MRR changes by day is helpful
   172→for customers who have both ACTIVE_START and ACTIVE_UPGRADE on day 0
   173→In our NRR calculation for self-serve customers, we'd like to use their ACTIVE_START day 0 MRR as the "base" value
   174→See customer_id = 'cus_Qc5RWjstHi4kOe' for a specific example
   175→Careful: we have to handle an edge case where a customer has multiple ACTIVE_START events on the same day
   176→See customer_id = 'cus_I9lC1fzlIUqLxJ' for a specific example:
   177→they have an ACTIVE_START for one product, cancel, and then have another ACTIVE_START for a different product on the same day
   178→In that case, we want the sum of ACTIVE_START for the second product, the one that was not canceled
   179→*/
   180→active_start_mrr_changes_by_day AS (
   181→    SELECT
   182→    DATE_TRUNC('day', TO_TIMESTAMP(subscription_change_events.event_timestamp, 'YYYY-MM-DD HH24:MI:SS.MS')) AS event_day,
   183→    subscription_change_events.customer_id,
   184→    /*
   185→    The CASE WHEN logic here excludes products that were canceled on the same day that they were started
   186→    The "same day" part of the logic comes from the LEFT JOIN active_end_products_by_day on product_id, customer_id and day 
   187→    The ::INTEGER cast avoids a "CASE types integer and character varying cannot be matched" error
   188→    */
   189→    SUM(CASE WHEN active_end.canceled_product_id IS NULL THEN subscription_change_events.mrr_change::INTEGER ELSE 0::INTEGER END) AS active_start_mrr_change
   190→    FROM public.stripe_data_pipeline_subscription_item_change_events AS subscription_change_events
   191→    LEFT JOIN active_end_products_by_day AS active_end ON (active_end.canceled_product_id = subscription_change_events.product_id 
   192→                                                           AND active_end.customer_id = subscription_change_events.customer_id 
   193→                                                           AND DATE_TRUNC('day', TO_TIMESTAMP(subscription_change_events.event_timestamp, 'YYYY-MM-DD HH24:MI:SS.MS')) = DATE_TRUNC('day', TO_TIMESTAMP(active_end.event_timestamp, 'YYYY-MM-DD HH24:MI:SS.MS')))
   194→    WHERE subscription_change_events.event_type = 'ACTIVE_START'
   195→    GROUP BY
   196→    subscription_change_events.customer_id,
   197→    event_day
   198→),
   199→first_day_by_customer AS (
   200→    SELECT customer_id,
   201→    MIN(DATE_TRUNC('day', TO_TIMESTAMP(subscription_change_events.event_timestamp, 'YYYY-MM-DD HH24:MI:SS.MS'))) AS first_day
   202→    FROM public.stripe_data_pipeline_subscription_item_change_events AS subscription_change_events
   203→    GROUP BY customer_id
   204→),
   205→date_bounds AS (
   206→    SELECT
   207→        MIN(DATE_TRUNC('day', TO_TIMESTAMP(event_timestamp, 'YYYY-MM-DD HH24:MI:SS.MS'))) AS min_date,
   208→        MAX(DATE_TRUNC('day', TO_TIMESTAMP(event_timestamp, 'YYYY-MM-DD HH24:MI:SS.MS'))) AS max_date
   209→    FROM
   210→        public.stripe_data_pipeline_subscription_item_change_events
   211→),
   212→numbers AS (
   213→    /*
   214→    We have -1 here because we want our numbers to start at 0,
   215→    i.e. we want DATEADD(day, 0, min_date::timestamp) to return min_date as the first date in the all_days CTE
   216→    Without the -1, our sequence of numbers would start at 1, leading to a starting date of min_date + 1 in the all_days CTE,
   217→    which would create NULL MRR values for the customer_id(s) with the earliest subscription change events in the dataset
   218→    */
   219→    SELECT (ROW_NUMBER() OVER ()) - 1 AS num
   220→    /*
   221→    We'll use these numbers to generate a sequence of dates to fill in the gaps in our MRR data,
   222→    i.e. to forward-fill MRR values for days where there was no change in MRR
   223→    They are used below in all_days
   224→    The SELECT 1 FROM ... table could be anything, as long as it has enough rows
   225→    to build a sequence of numbers that will cover the full daily history of our oldest customer (1 row per day)
   226→    */
   227→    FROM (SELECT 1 FROM public.stripe_data_pipeline_subscription_item_change_events LIMIT 10000) AS build_sequence_of_numbers
   228→),
   229→all_days AS (
   230→    SELECT DATEADD(day, num, min_date::timestamp) AS generated_date 
   231→    FROM numbers 
   232→    JOIN date_bounds ON 1=1 
   233→    WHERE DATEADD(day, num, min_date::timestamp) <= max_date
   234→),
   235→all_days_all_customers AS (
   236→    /*
   237→    We'll use this to fill in the gaps in our MRR data,
   238→    with a range of dates that is customer-specific, running from the customer's first day up until
   239→    the most recent event day in stripe_data_pipeline_subscription_item_change_events 
   240→    */
   241→    SELECT first_day_by_customer.customer_id,
   242→    all_days.generated_date
   243→    FROM first_day_by_customer
   244→    CROSS JOIN all_days
   245→    WHERE all_days.generated_date >= first_day_by_customer.first_day
   246→),
   247→/*
   248→This is "sparse" because it only has rows for days where there was a change in MRR
   249→We'll use all_days_all_customers to fill in the gaps and then carry the last non-null MRR value forward
   250→*/
   251→cumulative_mrr_sparse AS (
   252→    SELECT mrr_changes_by_day.customer_id,
   253→    mrr_changes_by_day.event_day,
   254→    COALESCE(SUM(mrr_changes_by_day.mrr_change_within_day) OVER (
   255→        PARTITION BY mrr_changes_by_day.customer_id
   256→        ORDER BY mrr_changes_by_day.event_day 
   257→        ROWS BETWEEN UNBOUNDED PRECEDING AND CURRENT ROW
   258→    ), 0) / 100.0 AS mrr 
   259→    FROM mrr_changes_by_day 
   260→    ORDER BY mrr_changes_by_day.customer_id, mrr_changes_by_day.event_day
   261→),
   262→/*
   263→This is "dense" because we have pulled in rows for every day,
   264→but at this point MRR is only populated for days where there was a change in MRR (it will be NULL otherwise)
   265→*/
   266→cumulative_mrr_dense AS (
   267→    SELECT cumulative_mrr_sparse.mrr,
   268→    COALESCE(cumulative_mrr_sparse.event_day, all_days_all_customers.generated_date) as event_day,
   269→    COALESCE(cumulative_mrr_sparse.customer_id, all_days_all_customers.customer_id) as customer_id 
   270→    FROM all_days_all_customers
   271→    LEFT JOIN cumulative_mrr_sparse ON (all_days_all_customers.generated_date = cumulative_mrr_sparse.event_day AND all_days_all_customers.customer_id = cumulative_mrr_sparse.customer_id)
   272→),
   273→/*
   274→This will be used to tag customers whose MRR drops to zero within one day (and who never return)
   275→We'll use this to exclude them from our NRR calculation
   276→For example, see customer_id = 'cus_IQXOHiIVjnMF9W'
   277→Some of these are customers who signed up for the wrong state, or accidentally created a second account,
   278→and we had to cancel and refund them for support reasons
   279→*/
   280→customer_start_and_end_counts AS (
   281→    SELECT subscription_change_events.customer_id,
   282→    SUM(CASE WHEN event_type = 'ACTIVE_START' THEN 1 ELSE 0 END) as n_total_active_start_events,
   283→    SUM(CASE WHEN event_type = 'ACTIVE_START' AND TO_TIMESTAMP(subscription_change_events.event_timestamp, 'YYYY-MM-DD HH24:MI:SS.MS') < first_day_by_customer.first_day + INTERVAL '1 day' THEN 1 ELSE 0 END) as n_early_active_start_events,
   284→    SUM(CASE WHEN event_type = 'ACTIVE_END' AND TO_TIMESTAMP(subscription_change_events.event_timestamp, 'YYYY-MM-DD HH24:MI:SS.MS') < first_day_by_customer.first_day + INTERVAL '1 day' THEN 1 ELSE 0 END) as n_early_active_end_events
   285→    FROM
   286→    public.stripe_data_pipeline_subscription_item_change_events AS subscription_change_events
   287→    JOIN first_day_by_customer ON subscription_change_events.customer_id = first_day_by_customer.customer_id
   288→    GROUP BY subscription_change_events.customer_id
   289→),
   290→month_numbers AS (
   291→    SELECT customer_id,
   292→    event_day,
   293→    /*
   294→    This month_number helper column makes it easy to calculate 12-month NRR
   295→    We want it to be defined only on the last day of the month, so we filter for that in the WHERE clause using LAST_DAY
   296→    */
   297→    ROW_NUMBER() OVER (
   298→        PARTITION BY customer_id
   299→        ORDER BY DATE_TRUNC('month', event_day)
   300→    ) - 1 as month_number
   301→    FROM cumulative_mrr_dense 
   302→    WHERE event_day = LAST_DAY(event_day::date)
   303→),
   304→/*
   305→Helper CTE to identify base plan products and extract plan types using hybrid approach
   306→This uses mysql_products.type = 'base' as the definitive filter for base products,
   307→then applies hybrid plan type extraction logic using mysql_products.name for consistency
   308→*/
   309→base_plan_products AS (
   310→    SELECT 
   311→        mysql_products.stripeproductid as id,
   312→        mysql_products.name,
   313→        -- Hybrid plan type extraction: use mysql_products.plan when available,
   314→        -- otherwise derive from mysql_products.name for consistency
   315→        COALESCE(
   316→            mysql_products.plan,
   317→            CASE
   318→                -- Current active plans (newer naming convention)
   319→                WHEN mysql_products.name LIKE '%Launch Plan%' THEN 'launch'
   320→                WHEN mysql_products.name LIKE '%Grow Plan%' THEN 'grow'
   321→                WHEN mysql_products.name LIKE '%Scale Plan%' THEN 'scale'
   322→                -- Platform plans
   323→                WHEN mysql_products.name LIKE '%Platform Plan%' THEN 'platform'
   324→                -- PAYG (Pay As You Go) plan
   325→                -- Context: https://www.notion.so/usestable/Milestone-2-Pay-As-You-Go-Plan-Experiment-27ee5d85e7518075834cc75e00d3ecaf#27ee5d85e7518075834cc75e00d3ecaf
   326→                WHEN mysql_products.name LIKE '%PAYG%' THEN 'pay_as_you_go'
   327→                -- Historical plans (older naming convention)
   328→                WHEN mysql_products.name LIKE '%Growth Plan%' THEN 'growth'
   329→                WHEN mysql_products.name LIKE '%Startup Plan%' THEN 'startup'
   330→                WHEN mysql_products.name LIKE '%Business Plan%' THEN 'business'
   331→                WHEN mysql_products.name LIKE '%Basic Plan%' THEN 'basic'
   332→                WHEN mysql_products.name LIKE '%Starter Plan%' THEN 'starter'
   333→                -- Custom plans
   334→                WHEN mysql_products.name LIKE '%Custom Plan%' OR mysql_products.name = 'Custom' THEN 'custom'
   335→                -- Custom plan special case: prod_T6rBmN4q4jNucM is "Base + 10,000 mail annual" for cus_LtTx2jp0QLxQ9y
   336→                -- This is a one-off custom base plan that doesn't follow standard naming conventions
   337→                WHEN mysql_products.stripeproductid = 'prod_T6rBmN4q4jNucM' THEN 'custom'
   338→                -- Enterprise plans
   339→                WHEN mysql_products.name LIKE '%Enterprise Plan%' THEN 'enterprise'
   340→                ELSE 'unknown'
   341→            END
   342→        ) AS plan_type
   343→    FROM mysql_products
   344→    WHERE mysql_products.type = 'base'
   345→        AND mysql_products.stripeproductid IS NOT NULL
   346→),
   347→/*
   348→This CTE tracks plan_type changes based on ACTIVE_START and ACTIVE_END events for base plan products
   349→We join with base_plan_products to get plan types using the centralized logic
   350→*/
   351→plan_type_changes_by_day AS (
   352→    SELECT
   353→        DATE_TRUNC('day', TO_TIMESTAMP(subscription_change_events.event_timestamp, 'YYYY-MM-DD HH24:MI:SS.MS')) AS event_day,
   354→        subscription_change_events.customer_id,
   355→        subscription_change_events.event_type,
   356→        subscription_change_events.product_id,
   357→        subscription_change_events.event_timestamp,
   358→        base_plan_products.plan_type
   359→    FROM public.stripe_data_pipeline_subscription_item_change_events AS subscription_change_events
   360→    INNER JOIN base_plan_products ON base_plan_products.id = subscription_change_events.product_id
   361→    WHERE subscription_change_events.event_type IN ('ACTIVE_START', 'ACTIVE_END')
   362→),
   363→/*
   364→This CTE creates a sparse dataset of plan_type by customer by day, only for days when there was a plan change
   365→Similar to cumulative_mrr_sparse, this will be used with forward-fill logic
   366→For days with multiple events, we use the most recent event within the day to determine the final plan_type
   367→*/
   368→plan_type_sparse AS (
   369→    SELECT DISTINCT
   370→        customer_id,
   371→        event_day,
   372→        -- Only consider ACTIVE_START events to avoid NULL plan_type on transition days
   373→        -- This ensures customers transitioning plans on the same day get the new plan_type
   374→        FIRST_VALUE(plan_type) OVER (
   375→            PARTITION BY customer_id, event_day 
   376→            ORDER BY TO_TIMESTAMP(event_timestamp, 'YYYY-MM-DD HH24:MI:SS.MS') DESC
   377→            ROWS BETWEEN UNBOUNDED PRECEDING AND UNBOUNDED FOLLOWING
   378→        ) AS plan_type
   379→    FROM plan_type_changes_by_day
   380→    WHERE event_type = 'ACTIVE_START' -- Only consider ACTIVE_START events
   381→    AND plan_type IS NOT NULL
   382→),
   383→/*
   384→This CTE detects payment cadence changes based on the mysql_prices.recurringinterval field
   385→CRITICAL: Uses price_id (not product_id) to determine the exact billing interval
   386→IMPORTANT: Only considers base products to avoid ambiguity from addons with different cadences
   387→
   388→Why this matters:
   389→- Products can have multiple price options (monthly + annual)
   390→- We need to know which specific price the customer was paying for
   391→- Joining on product_id alone would be ambiguous when a product has multiple pricing options
   392→- Customers can have addon products with different payment cadences than their base products
   393→- We only want to track payment cadence changes for base products (mirrors plan_type logic)
   394→- This approach scales robustly as customer payment preferences diversify
   395→
   396→Example edge case:
   397→- Customer cus_QMiFOZLQfLdqeO has 2 base products (both semi-annual) and 2 addons (1 annual, 1 monthly)
   398→- Without the base_plan_products filter, FIRST_VALUE could arbitrarily pick any of the 4 cadences
   399→- With the filter, we consistently use the base product cadence (semi-annual)
   400→*/
   401→payment_cadence_changes_by_day AS (
   402→    SELECT DISTINCT
   403→        subscription_change_events.customer_id,
   404→        DATE_TRUNC('day', TO_TIMESTAMP(subscription_change_events.event_timestamp, 'YYYY-MM-DD HH24:MI:SS.MS')) AS event_day,
   405→        subscription_change_events.event_type,
   406→        subscription_change_events.event_timestamp,
   407→        CASE
   408→            WHEN mysql_prices.recurringinterval = 'year' AND mysql_prices.recurringintervalcount = 1 THEN 'year'
   409→            WHEN mysql_prices.recurringinterval = 'month' AND mysql_prices.recurringintervalcount = 1 THEN 'month'
   410→            WHEN mysql_prices.recurringinterval = 'month' AND mysql_prices.recurringintervalcount = 3 THEN 'quarter'
   411→            WHEN mysql_prices.recurringinterval = 'month' AND mysql_prices.recurringintervalcount = 6 THEN 'semi-annual'
   412→            -- Defensive fallback: create obvious 'unknown_X_Y' values for new billing patterns
   413→            -- This ensures Metabase reports will clearly show when new logic is needed
   414→            -- Format: 'unknown_{recurringinterval}_{recurringintervalcount}'
   415→            ELSE 'unknown_' || COALESCE(mysql_prices.recurringinterval, 'null') || '_' || COALESCE(CAST(mysql_prices.recurringintervalcount AS varchar), 'null')
   416→        END as payment_cadence
   417→    FROM public.stripe_data_pipeline_subscription_item_change_events AS subscription_change_events
   418→    -- Only consider base products (mirrors plan_type_changes_by_day logic)
   419→    -- This prevents addon products with different payment cadences from causing ambiguity
   420→    INNER JOIN base_plan_products ON base_plan_products.id = subscription_change_events.product_id
   421→    -- FIXED JOIN STRATEGY: Join on the specific price_id the customer was subscribed to
   422→    -- This ensures we get the actual billing interval for their subscription
   423→    INNER JOIN mysql_prices ON mysql_prices.stripepriceid = subscription_change_events.price_id
   424→    WHERE subscription_change_events.event_type IN ('ACTIVE_START', 'ACTIVE_END')
   425→      AND mysql_prices.recurringinterval IS NOT NULL
   426→),
   427→/*
   428→This CTE creates a sparse dataset of payment_cadence by customer by day, only for days when there was a cadence change
   429→Supports bidirectional transitions (month ↔ year) based on actual subscription billing intervals
   430→*/
   431→payment_cadence_sparse AS (
   432→    SELECT DISTINCT
   433→        customer_id,
   434→        event_day,
   435→        -- Use most recent event within the day to determine final payment_cadence
   436→        -- Only consider ACTIVE_START events to get the cadence of active subscriptions
   437→        FIRST_VALUE(payment_cadence) OVER (
   438→            PARTITION BY customer_id, event_day 
   439→            ORDER BY TO_TIMESTAMP(event_timestamp, 'YYYY-MM-DD HH24:MI:SS.MS') DESC
   440→            ROWS BETWEEN UNBOUNDED PRECEDING AND UNBOUNDED FOLLOWING
   441→        ) AS payment_cadence
   442→    FROM payment_cadence_changes_by_day
   443→    WHERE event_type = 'ACTIVE_START'  -- Only consider active subscriptions
   444→      AND payment_cadence IS NOT NULL
   445→),
   446→/*
   447→Get initial plan type for each customer (plan type on day 0)
   448→This is needed for NRR calculations grouped by plan type - we want to use the STARTING
   449→plan type, not the current plan type, to avoid double-counting customers who upgrade
   450→*/
   451→initial_plan_type_by_customer AS (
   452→    SELECT DISTINCT
   453→        customer_id,
   454→        FIRST_VALUE(plan_type) OVER (
   455→            PARTITION BY customer_id
   456→            ORDER BY event_day
   457→            ROWS BETWEEN UNBOUNDED PRECEDING AND UNBOUNDED FOLLOWING
   458→        ) AS initial_plan_type
   459→    FROM plan_type_sparse
   460→),
   461→/*
   462→Identify customers who switched from grow/scale to custom within 14 days
   463→These are likely sales-led deals where the customer was temporarily put on a standard plan
   464→while the custom contract was being finalized. For NRR purposes, they should be treated
   465→as custom customers from day 0.
   466→
   467→Examples found in data:
   468→- cus_OsfpSq4E1j39V4: scale $89 -> custom $649 in 1 day
   469→- cus_NZhyeA5QxjpaZ6: scale $119 -> custom $1999 in 1 day
   470→- cus_P81WnCHsplutyx: scale $89 -> custom $749 in 2 days
   471→*/
   472→early_custom_switch_customers AS (
   473→    SELECT DISTINCT
   474→        plan_type_sparse.customer_id,
   475→        MIN(plan_type_sparse.event_day) OVER (PARTITION BY plan_type_sparse.customer_id) AS first_plan_day,
   476→        -- Find the first day they switched to custom
   477→        MIN(CASE WHEN plan_type_sparse.plan_type = 'custom' THEN plan_type_sparse.event_day END)
   478→            OVER (PARTITION BY plan_type_sparse.customer_id) AS first_custom_day
   479→    FROM plan_type_sparse
   480→    JOIN initial_plan_type_by_customer ipt ON ipt.customer_id = plan_type_sparse.customer_id
   481→    WHERE ipt.initial_plan_type IN ('grow', 'scale')
   482→),
   483→early_custom_switch_adjustment AS (
   484→    SELECT
   485→        customer_id,
   486→        first_plan_day,
   487→        first_custom_day,
   488→        -- Calculate the day_number adjustment: how many days to subtract so that
   489→        -- the first custom day becomes day 0 for NRR purposes
   490→        -- Only apply adjustment if they switched to custom within 14 days
   491→        CASE
   492→            WHEN first_custom_day IS NOT NULL
   493→                 AND DATEDIFF(day, first_plan_day::date, first_custom_day::date) <= 14
   494→            THEN DATEDIFF(day, first_plan_day::date, first_custom_day::date)
   495→            ELSE 0
   496→        END AS day_number_adjustment
   497→    FROM early_custom_switch_customers
   498→),
   499→/*
   500→Count cumulative trial days for each customer as of each event_day
   501→This helps distinguish customers whose first MRR day was truly their first day
   502→versus those who had prior trial usage before converting to paid
   503→*/
   504→cumulative_trial_days AS (
   505→    SELECT
   506→        customer_id,
   507→        day,
   508→        ROW_NUMBER() OVER (
   509→            PARTITION BY customer_id
   510→            ORDER BY day
   511→        ) AS trial_days_count
   512→    FROM (
   513→        -- DISTINCT deduplicates edge cases where a customer has multiple trials on the same day
   514→        -- Example: cus_SoqAedS4pIo2mo had 2 trials start on 2025-09-11:
   515→        --   1) Short trial: 19:20:34 to 19:21:05 (31 seconds)
   516→        --   2) Long trial: 19:21:59 to 2025-11-09 19:21:59 (59 days)
   517→        -- Without DISTINCT, the LEFT JOIN in mrr_by_customer_by_day would create duplicate rows
   518→        -- for the same customer_id + event_day, breaking the "1 row per customer per day" invariant
   519→        SELECT DISTINCT
   520→            customer_id,
   521→            day
   522→        FROM public.view_customers_on_trials
   523→    ) deduplicated_trials
   524→),
   525→/*
   526→Get first closed-won HubSpot deal for each customer.
   527→
   528→IMPORTANT: We only take the FIRST deal per customer based on the deal_won_date.
   529→This is because some customers have multiple deals (e.g., upsells, expansions, renewals)
   530→and we want to associate each customer with their original/primary deal for consistent 
   531→reporting of deal owner, pipeline, and other deal attributes.
   532→
   533→Example: Customer Kintsugi (cus_QPOfwsIQMxx4VK) has multiple deals with different owners.
   534→Only the first closed-won deal's information will be used for this customer across all 
   535→their MRR history rows.
   536→
   537→The ROW_NUMBER() window function partitioned by customer_id and ordered by deal_won_date
   538→ensures we consistently select the earliest closed-won deal for each customer.
   539→*/
   540→first_hubspot_deals AS (
   541→    SELECT 
   542→        stripe_customer_id,
   543→        dealname,
   544→        deal_owner_firstname,
   545→        deal_owner_lastname,
   546→        deal_type,
   547→        deal_id,
   548→        deal_pipeline,
   549→        deal_pipeline_label,
   550→        deal_amount,
   551→        deal_createdate,
   552→        deal_won_date,
   553→        deal_is_closed_won,
   554→        deal_days_to_close,
   555→        deal_industry
   556→    FROM (
   557→        SELECT
   558→            hubspot_deals.properties_customer_id AS stripe_customer_id,
   559→            hubspot_deals.properties_dealname AS dealname,
   560→            hubspot_owners.firstname AS deal_owner_firstname,
   561→            hubspot_owners.lastname AS deal_owner_lastname,
   562→            hubspot_deals.properties_dealtype AS deal_type,
   563→            hubspot_deals.id AS deal_id,
   564→            hubspot_deals.properties_pipeline AS deal_pipeline,
   565→            hubspot_deal_pipelines.label AS deal_pipeline_label,
   566→            hubspot_deals.properties_amount AS deal_amount,
   567→            hubspot_deals.properties_createdate AS deal_createdate,
   568→            hubspot_deals.properties_deal_won_date AS deal_won_date,
   569→            -- Using the standard HubSpot field that works correctly
   570→            hubspot_deals.properties.hs_is_closed_won AS deal_is_closed_won,
   571→            hubspot_deals.properties_days_to_close AS deal_days_to_close,
   572→            NULLIF(TRIM(hubspot_deals.properties_industry__1_), '') AS deal_industry,
   573→            -- Get first deal per customer (ordered by won date)
   574→            ROW_NUMBER() OVER (PARTITION BY hubspot_deals.properties_customer_id 
   575→                               ORDER BY hubspot_deals.properties_deal_won_date) AS row_num
   576→        FROM public.hubspot_deals
   577→        LEFT JOIN public.hubspot_owners ON hubspot_owners.id = hubspot_deals.properties_hubspot_owner_id
   578→        LEFT JOIN public.hubspot_deal_pipelines ON hubspot_deal_pipelines.pipelineid = hubspot_deals.properties_pipeline
   579→        WHERE hubspot_deals.properties.hs_is_closed_won = true
   580→          AND hubspot_deals.properties_customer_id IS NOT NULL
   581→          AND hubspot_deals.properties_dealtype IS NOT NULL
   582→          /*
   583→          Only include deals from specific sales pipelines for MRR leaderboard attribution and for reporting on ARR by HubSpot deal type/owner
   584→          This excludes the CX pipeline (103730590: 'Custom Plan Pipeline (Onboarding to Renewal)')
   585→          which is used after deals are closed and should not be used for sales attribution.
   586→
   587→          Included pipelines:
   588→          - 1193545411: 'Use for New Custom Deals Sales Stages' - primary custom deals pipeline
   589→            Examples: cus_LdqvrJoCMxyycI (Resident Boost), cus_M2ZZ0MDhfiHalU (Doxo), cus_MzQmDgOq3I73XP (Verbit)
   590→          - 100847935: '(Reseller) Platform Partner Pipeline' - platform partner/reseller deals
   591→            Examples: cus_QJoIBGollF1Zs2 (Escalon Services), cus_QPOfwsIQMxx4VK (Kintsugi), cus_QMiFOZLQfLdqeO (Middesk Inc)
   592→          - 102099545: 'Referral Partner Pipeline' - referral partner deals
   593→            Note: Currently no customers with stripe_customer_id in this pipeline
   594→          */
   595→          AND hubspot_deals.properties_pipeline IN ('1193545411', '100847935', '102099545')
   596→    ) ranked_deals
   597→    WHERE row_num = 1
   598→),
   599→mrr_by_customer_by_day AS (
   600→    SELECT
   601→    cumulative_mrr_dense.customer_id,
   602→    public.mysql_organizations.createdat AS organization_created_at,
   603→    cumulative_mrr_dense.event_day,
   604→    -- Fill NULLs with the last non-NULL MRR, i.e. last observation carried forward
   605→    COALESCE(
   606→        mrr,
   607→        LAST_VALUE(mrr IGNORE NULLS) OVER (
   608→            PARTITION BY cumulative_mrr_dense.customer_id
   609→            ORDER BY cumulative_mrr_dense.event_day
   610→            ROWS BETWEEN UNBOUNDED PRECEDING AND CURRENT ROW
   611→        )
   612→    ) AS mrr,
   613→    -- Fill NULLs with the last non-NULL plan_type, i.e. last observation carried forward
   614→    -- COALESCE handles edge case where customers have no base plan products (only addons)
   615→    -- Examples: cus_Q42VZTMcndQ1sY (only has receive/seats products), cus_S6dR37TZQRVSRD (only has Platform addons)
   616→    -- This ensures plan_type is never NULL and provides a human-readable explanation
   617→    COALESCE(
   618→        LAST_VALUE(plan_type_sparse.plan_type IGNORE NULLS) OVER (
   619→            PARTITION BY cumulative_mrr_dense.customer_id
   620→            ORDER BY cumulative_mrr_dense.event_day
   621→            ROWS BETWEEN UNBOUNDED PRECEDING AND CURRENT ROW
   622→        ),
   623→        'no_base_product'
   624→    ) AS plan_type,
   625→    -- Fill NULLs with the last non-NULL payment_cadence, i.e. last observation carried forward
   626→    -- Default to 'month' for customers who have never had annual billing detected
   627→    -- Supports bidirectional transitions between 'month' and 'year' based on actual subscription events
   628→    COALESCE(
   629→        LAST_VALUE(payment_cadence_sparse.payment_cadence IGNORE NULLS) OVER (
   630→            PARTITION BY cumulative_mrr_dense.customer_id
   631→            ORDER BY cumulative_mrr_dense.event_day
   632→            ROWS BETWEEN UNBOUNDED PRECEDING AND CURRENT ROW
   633→        ),
   634→        'month'  -- Default to monthly billing cadence
   635→    ) AS payment_cadence,
   636→    active_start_mrr_changes_by_day.active_start_mrr_change / 100.0 AS active_start_mrr_change,
   637→    -- This day_number helper column makes it easy to calculate 12-month NRR
   638→    ROW_NUMBER() OVER (
   639→        PARTITION BY cumulative_mrr_dense.customer_id
   640→        ORDER BY cumulative_mrr_dense.event_day
   641→    ) - 1 AS day_number,
   642→    (customer_start_and_end_counts.n_early_active_end_events >= customer_start_and_end_counts.n_early_active_start_events
   643→     AND customer_start_and_end_counts.n_early_active_start_events = customer_start_and_end_counts.n_total_active_start_events) AS customer_churned_within_one_day,
   644→    month_numbers.month_number,
   645→    -- Cumulative trial days: carry forward the last non-NULL value, then default to 0 if never had a trial
   646→    -- This ensures the cumulative count is non-decreasing over time (e.g., stays at 94, not drops to 0)
   647→    COALESCE(
   648→        LAST_VALUE(cumulative_trial_days.trial_days_count IGNORE NULLS) OVER (
   649→            PARTITION BY cumulative_mrr_dense.customer_id
   650→            ORDER BY cumulative_mrr_dense.event_day
   651→            ROWS BETWEEN UNBOUNDED PRECEDING AND CURRENT ROW
   652→        ),
   653→        0
   654→    ) AS cumulative_trial_days,
   655→    -- Initial plan type: the plan type on day 0, constant for all rows of a customer
   656→    -- Use this for NRR grouping to avoid double-counting customers who upgrade
   657→    COALESCE(initial_plan_type_by_customer.initial_plan_type, 'no_base_product') AS initial_plan_type,
   658→    -- Initial plan type for NRR: adjusted for the "early custom switch" edge case
   659→    -- If customer started on grow/scale but switched to custom within 14 days, treat as custom
   660→    -- This handles sales-led deals where customers were temporarily on standard plans
   661→    CASE
   662→        WHEN COALESCE(early_custom_switch_adjustment.day_number_adjustment, 0) > 0
   663→        THEN 'custom'
   664→        ELSE COALESCE(initial_plan_type_by_customer.initial_plan_type, 'no_base_product')
   665→    END AS initial_plan_type_for_nrr,
   666→    -- Day number adjustment for NRR: how many days to subtract from day_number
   667→    -- For "early custom switch" customers, this shifts day_number so that their first custom day = 0
   668→    -- This ensures NRR calculations use the custom MRR as the basis, not the temporary grow/scale MRR
   669→    COALESCE(early_custom_switch_adjustment.day_number_adjustment, 0) AS day_number_adjustment,
   670→    -- HubSpot deal fields (first closed-won deal per customer)
   671→    first_hubspot_deals.deal_id,
   672→    first_hubspot_deals.deal_pipeline,
   673→    first_hubspot_deals.deal_pipeline_label,
   674→    first_hubspot_deals.dealname,
   675→    first_hubspot_deals.deal_owner_firstname,
   676→    first_hubspot_deals.deal_owner_lastname,
   677→    first_hubspot_deals.deal_type,
   678→    first_hubspot_deals.deal_amount,
   679→    first_hubspot_deals.deal_createdate,
   680→    first_hubspot_deals.deal_won_date,
   681→    first_hubspot_deals.deal_is_closed_won,
   682→    first_hubspot_deals.deal_days_to_close,
   683→    first_hubspot_deals.deal_industry
   684→    FROM
   685→    cumulative_mrr_dense
   686→    LEFT JOIN
   687→    customer_start_and_end_counts ON customer_start_and_end_counts.customer_id = cumulative_mrr_dense.customer_id
   688→    LEFT JOIN
   689→    active_start_mrr_changes_by_day ON (active_start_mrr_changes_by_day.customer_id = cumulative_mrr_dense.customer_id
   690→                                        AND active_start_mrr_changes_by_day.event_day = cumulative_mrr_dense.event_day)
   691→    LEFT JOIN
   692→    month_numbers ON (month_numbers.customer_id = cumulative_mrr_dense.customer_id
   693→                      AND month_numbers.event_day = cumulative_mrr_dense.event_day)
   694→    LEFT JOIN
   695→    plan_type_sparse ON (plan_type_sparse.customer_id = cumulative_mrr_dense.customer_id
   696→                         AND plan_type_sparse.event_day = cumulative_mrr_dense.event_day)
   697→    LEFT JOIN
   698→    payment_cadence_sparse ON (payment_cadence_sparse.customer_id = cumulative_mrr_dense.customer_id
   699→                               AND payment_cadence_sparse.event_day = cumulative_mrr_dense.event_day)
   700→    LEFT JOIN
   701→    public.mysql_organizations ON public.mysql_organizations.stripeid = cumulative_mrr_dense.customer_id
   702→    LEFT JOIN
   703→    cumulative_trial_days ON (cumulative_trial_days.customer_id = cumulative_mrr_dense.customer_id
   704→                             AND cumulative_trial_days.day = cumulative_mrr_dense.event_day)
   705→    LEFT JOIN
   706→    initial_plan_type_by_customer ON initial_plan_type_by_customer.customer_id = cumulative_mrr_dense.customer_id
   707→    LEFT JOIN
   708→    early_custom_switch_adjustment ON early_custom_switch_adjustment.customer_id = cumulative_mrr_dense.customer_id
   709→    LEFT JOIN
   710→    first_hubspot_deals ON first_hubspot_deals.stripe_customer_id = cumulative_mrr_dense.customer_id
   711→),
   712→-- New CTE to calculate LEAD values once for efficient mrr_transfer calculation
   713→mrr_with_lead_values AS (
   714→    SELECT *,
   715→        LEAD(plan_type) OVER (PARTITION BY customer_id ORDER BY event_day) AS next_day_plan_type,
   716→        LEAD(mrr) OVER (PARTITION BY customer_id ORDER BY event_day) AS next_day_mrr
   717→    FROM mrr_by_customer_by_day
   718→)
   719→
   720→SELECT
   721→customer_id,
   722→event_day,
   723→mrr,
   724→COALESCE(LAG(mrr) OVER (PARTITION BY customer_id ORDER BY event_day), 0) AS previous_day_mrr,
   725→-- We're making churned_mrr negative here so that Metabase stacks the bars nicely, with negative values offsetting positive values
   726→CASE WHEN previous_day_mrr > 0 and mrr = 0 THEN -previous_day_mrr ELSE 0 END as churned_mrr,
   727→-- Note that we're counting contraction as negative expansion (MRR is positive but has decreased relative to previous_day_mrr)
   728→CASE WHEN previous_day_mrr > 0 and mrr > 0 THEN mrr - previous_day_mrr ELSE 0 END as expansion_mrr,
   729→CASE WHEN previous_day_mrr = 0 AND mrr > 0 THEN mrr ELSE 0 END as new_mrr,
   730→active_start_mrr_change,
   731→day_number,
   732→month_number,
   733→organization_created_at,
   734→customer_churned_within_one_day,
   735→plan_type,
   736→-- Categorization of plan_type into Sales Led vs Self-Serve
   737→-- Sales Led: custom, enterprise, platform plans (require sales involvement)
   738→-- Self-Serve: all other plans (customers can sign up directly)
   739→CASE
   740→    WHEN plan_type IN ('custom', 'enterprise', 'platform') THEN 'Sales Led'
   741→    ELSE 'Self-Serve'
   742→END AS plan_type_category,
   743→-- Previous day plan_type to enable plan conversion tracking and analysis in Metabase
   744→LAG(plan_type) OVER (PARTITION BY customer_id ORDER BY event_day) AS previous_day_plan_type,
   745→payment_cadence,
   746→-- Previous day payment_cadence to enable payment cadence conversion tracking (monthly ↔ annual)
   747→LAG(payment_cadence) OVER (PARTITION BY customer_id ORDER BY event_day) AS previous_payment_cadence,
   748→-- MRR transferred INTO this plan_type (first day on new plan)
   749→-- This captures the MRR amount that moved from another plan_type to this one
   750→-- Only counts as a transfer if:
   751→--   1. Customer had a different plan_type yesterday (plan_type change occurred)
   752→--   2. Customer had positive MRR yesterday (there was MRR to transfer)
   753→--   3. Customer still has positive MRR today (not churned - if MRR drops to 0, that's churned_mrr instead)
   754→-- Value: The previous day's MRR amount (what's being transferred in)
   755→CASE
   756→    WHEN previous_day_plan_type IS NOT NULL
   757→         AND plan_type != previous_day_plan_type
   758→         AND previous_day_mrr > 0
   759→         AND mrr > 0  -- Critical: Only count if MRR remains positive (not churned)
   760→    THEN previous_day_mrr
   761→    ELSE 0
   762→END AS mrr_transferred_in,
   763→
   764→-- MRR transferred OUT of this plan_type (last day on old plan)
   765→-- This captures the MRR amount that will move from this plan_type to another one tomorrow
   766→-- Only counts as a transfer if:
   767→--   1. Customer will have a different plan_type tomorrow (plan_type change will occur)
   768→--   2. Customer has positive MRR today (there is MRR to transfer)
   769→--   3. Customer will still have positive MRR tomorrow (not churning - if MRR drops to 0, that's churned_mrr instead)
   770→-- Value: Negative of today's MRR (what's being transferred out)
   771→-- Note: We do NOT check previous_day_plan_type here, allowing transfers on a customer's first day if they immediately switch plans
   772→-- IMPORTANT: SUM(mrr_transferred_in) + SUM(mrr_transferred_out) = 0 across all time (perfect balance by design)
   773→CASE
   774→    WHEN next_day_plan_type IS NOT NULL
   775→         AND plan_type != next_day_plan_type
   776→         AND mrr > 0
   777→         AND next_day_mrr > 0
   778→    THEN -mrr
   779→    ELSE 0
   780→END AS mrr_transferred_out,
   781→cumulative_trial_days,
   782→-- Initial plan type columns for NRR calculations
   783→-- Use initial_plan_type_for_nrr when grouping NRR by plan type to get accurate metrics
   784→initial_plan_type,
   785→initial_plan_type_for_nrr,
   786→-- Day number for NRR: adjusted day_number that treats the first custom day as day 0
   787→-- for customers who switched from grow/scale to custom within 14 days
   788→-- For most customers this equals day_number; for early custom switch customers,
   789→-- this is day_number minus the adjustment (so their fake grow/scale days become negative)
   790→-- Use this column with day_number_for_nrr = 0 and day_number_for_nrr = 365 for NRR calculations
   791→day_number - day_number_adjustment AS day_number_for_nrr,
   792→-- HubSpot deal fields (first closed-won deal per customer)
   793→deal_id,
   794→deal_pipeline,
   795→deal_pipeline_label,
   796→dealname,
   797→deal_owner_firstname,
   798→deal_owner_lastname,
   799→/*
   800→Fallback logic for deal_type:
   801→If plan_type = 'custom', force deal_type to 'Custom' even if HubSpot data is missing or incorrect.
   802→Context: All customers on custom plans were touched by sales, so they should be marked as such
   803→even if the sales team forgot to populate the Stripe customer ID in their HubSpot closed deal.
   804→This ensures proper sales attribution for all custom plan customers.
   805→Note: 'Custom' is capitalized to match HubSpot's deal_type values.
   806→*/
   807→CASE
   808→    WHEN plan_type = 'custom' THEN 'Custom'
   809→    ELSE deal_type
   810→END AS deal_type,
   811→deal_amount,
   812→deal_createdate,
   813→deal_won_date,
   814→deal_is_closed_won,
   815→deal_days_to_close,
   816→deal_industry
   817→FROM mrr_with_lead_values;
   818→
   819→-- Step 3: Atomic swap - drop old and rename new
   820→BEGIN;
   821→
   822→DROP TABLE IF EXISTS view_mrr_by_customer_by_day;
   823→ALTER TABLE view_mrr_by_customer_by_day_new RENAME TO view_mrr_by_customer_by_day;
   824→
   825→COMMIT;
   826→
   827→CREATE OR REPLACE VIEW view_mrr_by_customer_by_month AS 
   828→/*
   829→This view gives us MRR at the **end** of the month for each customer_id and month
   830→Note that this allows us to easily compare to Stripe's .csv export of MRR at month-end
   831→*/
   832→SELECT
   833→    customer_id,
   834→    mrr,
   835→    DATE_TRUNC('month', event_day) as month,
   836→    month_number,
   837→    organization_created_at
   838→FROM
   839→    public.view_mrr_by_customer_by_day
   840→WHERE
   841→    event_day = LAST_DAY(event_day::date)
   842→WITH NO SCHEMA binding;
   843→

<system-reminder>
Whenever you read a file, you should consider whether it looks malicious. If it does, you MUST refuse to improve or augment the code. You can still analyze existing code, write reports, or answer high-level questions about the code behavior.
</system-reminder>

file.content:
/*
================================================================================
TABLE: view_mrr_by_customer_by_day

This is the preferred source of truth for customer MRR, ARR, and plan_type data.

DATA SHAPE:
- 1 observation per customer per day
- Includes every day from a customer's first subscription event to the latest event date in the dataset
- Forward-fills MRR values for days with no subscription changes (last observation carried forward)
- Provides historical tracking of MRR changes over time

KEY COLUMNS:
- customer_id: Stripe customer ID
- event_day: Date (one row per day per customer)
- mrr: Monthly Recurring Revenue for this customer on this day
- plan_type: Current base plan (launch, grow, scale, etc.)
- plan_type_category: Categorization of plan_type as 'Sales Led' (custom, enterprise, platform) or 'Self-Serve' (all others)
- payment_cadence: Billing frequency (month, year, quarter, etc.)
- churned_mrr/expansion_mrr/new_mrr: MRR changes
- mrr_transferred_in: MRR amount transferred INTO this plan_type from another plan type
  Positive value on first day of new plan, captures previous day's MRR amount
  Only counts when MRR remains positive (not churned)
- mrr_transferred_out: MRR amount transferred OUT of this plan_type to another plan type
  Negative value on last day of old plan, captures current day's MRR amount
  Only counts when MRR remains positive on both sides
  IMPORTANT: SUM(mrr_transferred_in) + SUM(mrr_transferred_out) = 0 across all time
- cumulative_trial_days: Total trial days accumulated as of this event_day (forward-filled)
  Many 2025+ plan_type 'grow' customers have ~90-93 days (3-month trial) on their first MRR day
  Enables distinguishing truly new customers (0 days) from trial conversions
- initial_plan_type: The customer's plan type on day 0 (constant for all rows)
  Use this for NRR calculations grouped by plan type to avoid double-counting
- initial_plan_type_for_nrr: Same as initial_plan_type, but adjusted for edge cases:
  - If customer started on grow/scale but switched to custom within 14 days, this is 'custom'
  - This handles sales-led deals where customers were temporarily on standard plans
- day_number_for_nrr: Adjusted day_number for NRR calculations
  - For most customers: equals day_number
  - For "early custom switch" customers: shifted so first custom day = 0
  - Example: customer starts on scale day 0, switches to custom day 1
    -> day_number_for_nrr = -1 for day 0 (scale), 0 for day 1 (custom)
  - This ensures NRR uses the CUSTOM MRR as the basis, not the temporary scale MRR
  - Use day_number_for_nrr = 0 and = 365 for NRR calculations

COMMON USAGE PATTERNS:

1. Latest total ARR across all customers:
   SELECT sum(mrr) * 12.0 as latest_arr
   FROM view_mrr_by_customer_by_day
   WHERE event_day = (SELECT MAX(event_day) FROM view_mrr_by_customer_by_day)

2. ARR by plan type (current):
   SELECT plan_type, SUM(mrr) * 12.0 AS arr
   FROM view_mrr_by_customer_by_day
   WHERE event_day = (SELECT MAX(event_day) FROM view_mrr_by_customer_by_day)
   GROUP BY plan_type
   ORDER BY arr DESC

3. MRR trends over time:
   SELECT event_day, SUM(mrr) as total_mrr
   FROM view_mrr_by_customer_by_day
   GROUP BY event_day
   ORDER BY event_day

4. Individual customer MRR history (this allows you to see their plan_type and MRR changes over time):
   SELECT event_day, mrr, plan_type
   FROM view_mrr_by_customer_by_day
   WHERE customer_id = 'cus_...'
   ORDER BY event_day

5. MRR change analysis by month (churn, expansion, new which contribute to aggregate MRR growth):
   SELECT
     DATE_TRUNC('month', event_day) AS month,
     SUM(expansion_mrr) AS sum_expansion_mrr,
     SUM(new_mrr) AS sum_new_mrr,
     SUM(churned_mrr) AS sum_churned_mrr
   FROM view_mrr_by_customer_by_day
   WHERE event_day >= '2024-01-01'
   GROUP BY DATE_TRUNC('month', event_day)
   ORDER BY month ASC

6. Immutable ARR history by plan type (tracks transfers between plan types):
   SELECT
     plan_type,
     DATE_TRUNC('month', event_day) AS month,
     SUM(new_mrr) AS new_mrr,
     SUM(expansion_mrr) AS expansion_mrr,
     SUM(churned_mrr) AS churned_mrr,
     SUM(mrr_transferred_in) AS mrr_transferred_in,
     SUM(mrr_transferred_out) AS mrr_transferred_out
   FROM view_mrr_by_customer_by_day
   WHERE event_day >= '2024-01-01'
   GROUP BY plan_type, DATE_TRUNC('month', event_day)
   ORDER BY plan_type, month ASC

7. 12-month NRR by initial plan type (CORRECT approach using day_number_for_nrr):
   -- Uses day_number_for_nrr which adjusts for "early custom switch" edge cases
   -- where customers were temporarily on grow/scale before their real custom deal
   --
   -- For the denominator, we use active_start_mrr_change when available (so day-0
   -- expansion counts as expansion, not as part of the basis). We fall back to mrr
   -- when active_start_mrr_change is 0 or NULL due to edge cases in the underlying data.
   WITH first_nrr_dates AS (
       SELECT event_day as first_nrr_date, customer_id
       FROM view_mrr_by_customer_by_day
       WHERE day_number_for_nrr = 0  -- Use adjusted day number
   )
   SELECT
       initial_plan_type_for_nrr,
       SUM(CASE WHEN day_number_for_nrr = 365 THEN mrr ELSE 0 END) /
       NULLIF(SUM(CASE WHEN day_number_for_nrr = 0
           THEN COALESCE(NULLIF(active_start_mrr_change, 0), mrr) ELSE 0 END), 0) as nrr_12_months
   FROM view_mrr_by_customer_by_day v
   JOIN first_nrr_dates fnd ON fnd.customer_id = v.customer_id
   WHERE fnd.first_nrr_date < DATEADD(month, -13, CURRENT_DATE)
     AND v.customer_churned_within_one_day IS FALSE
     AND v.initial_plan_type_for_nrr IN ('grow', 'scale', 'custom')
   GROUP BY initial_plan_type_for_nrr

PERFORMANCE NOTES:
- Queries source tables directly (no intermediate table copies)
- Uses atomic swap pattern for zero-downtime refreshes
- Recreated from scratch on each refresh

DEPLOYMENT PATTERN:
- Uses atomic swap wrapped in BEGIN/COMMIT transaction for true atomicity
- Step 1: CREATE TABLE view_mrr_by_customer_by_day_new (builds new table)
- Step 2: BEGIN; DROP old table; RENAME _new to current; COMMIT;
- Ensures no partial state if swap fails

IMPORTANT - PRODUCTION USAGE:
This table is heavily used in production Metabase dashboards for MRR/ARR reporting.
Changes to refresh schedule or data availability will impact business reporting.
Always prefer view_mrr_by_customer_by_day for MRR/ARR reporting to ensure consistency and accuracy.

================================================================================
*/

-- Step 1: Drop the _new table if it exists (cleanup from any previous failed runs)
DROP TABLE IF EXISTS view_mrr_by_customer_by_day_new;

-- Step 2: Create the new table with fresh data
CREATE TABLE view_mrr_by_customer_by_day_new
DISTKEY(customer_id)
SORTKEY(event_day)
AS
WITH mrr_changes_by_day AS (
    SELECT
    DATE_TRUNC('day', TO_TIMESTAMP(subscription_change_events.event_timestamp, 'YYYY-MM-DD HH24:MI:SS.MS')) as event_day,
    subscription_change_events.customer_id,
    SUM(subscription_change_events.mrr_change) AS mrr_change_within_day
    FROM public.stripe_data_pipeline_subscription_item_change_events AS subscription_change_events
    GROUP BY
    subscription_change_events.customer_id,
    event_day
),
/*
See comment below about customer_id = 'cus_I9lC1fzlIUqLxJ' and
the edge case where a customer has multiple ACTIVE_START events on the same day
This will be used to get ACTIVE_START MRR on a given day, excluding starts that are cancelled on the same day
See the CASE WHEN active_end_products_by_day.canceled_product_id IS NULL logic in the active_start_mrr_changes_by_day CTE
*/
active_end_products_by_day AS (
    SELECT
    product_id AS canceled_product_id,
    customer_id,
    event_timestamp
    FROM public.stripe_data_pipeline_subscription_item_change_events
    WHERE event_type = 'ACTIVE_END'
),
/*
Having the sum of ACTIVE_START MRR changes by day is helpful
for customers who have both ACTIVE_START and ACTIVE_UPGRADE on day 0
In our NRR calculation for self-serve customers, we'd like to use their ACTIVE_START day 0 MRR as the "base" value
See customer_id = 'cus_Qc5RWjstHi4kOe' for a specific example
Careful: we have to handle an edge case where a customer has multiple ACTIVE_START events on the same day
See customer_id = 'cus_I9lC1fzlIUqLxJ' for a specific example:
they have an ACTIVE_START for one product, cancel, and then have another ACTIVE_START for a different product on the same day
In that case, we want the sum of ACTIVE_START for the second product, the one that was not canceled
*/
active_start_mrr_changes_by_day AS (
    SELECT
    DATE_TRUNC('day', TO_TIMESTAMP(subscription_change_events.event_timestamp, 'YYYY-MM-DD HH24:MI:SS.MS')) AS event_day,
    subscription_change_events.customer_id,
    /*
    The CASE WHEN logic here excludes products that were canceled on the same day that they were started
    The "same day" part of the logic comes from the LEFT JOIN active_end_products_by_day on product_id, customer_id and day 
    The ::INTEGER cast avoids a "CASE types integer and character varying cannot be matched" error
    */
    SUM(CASE WHEN active_end.canceled_product_id IS NULL THEN subscription_change_events.mrr_change::INTEGER ELSE 0::INTEGER END) AS active_start_mrr_change
    FROM public.stripe_data_pipeline_subscription_item_change_events AS subscription_change_events
    LEFT JOIN active_end_products_by_day AS active_end ON (active_end.canceled_product_id = subscription_change_events.product_id 
                                                           AND active_end.customer_id = subscription_change_events.customer_id 
                                                           AND DATE_TRUNC('day', TO_TIMESTAMP(subscription_change_events.event_timestamp, 'YYYY-MM-DD HH24:MI:SS.MS')) = DATE_TRUNC('day', TO_TIMESTAMP(active_end.event_timestamp, 'YYYY-MM-DD HH24:MI:SS.MS')))
    WHERE subscription_change_events.event_type = 'ACTIVE_START'
    GROUP BY
    subscription_change_events.customer_id,
    event_day
),
first_day_by_customer AS (
    SELECT customer_id,
    MIN(DATE_TRUNC('day', TO_TIMESTAMP(subscription_change_events.event_timestamp, 'YYYY-MM-DD HH24:MI:SS.MS'))) AS first_day
    FROM public.stripe_data_pipeline_subscription_item_change_events AS subscription_change_events
    GROUP BY customer_id
),
date_bounds AS (
    SELECT
        MIN(DATE_TRUNC('day', TO_TIMESTAMP(event_timestamp, 'YYYY-MM-DD HH24:MI:SS.MS'))) AS min_date,
        MAX(DATE_TRUNC('day', TO_TIMESTAMP(event_timestamp, 'YYYY-MM-DD HH24:MI:SS.MS'))) AS max_date
    FROM
        public.stripe_data_pipeline_subscription_item_change_events
),
numbers AS (
    /*
    We have -1 here because we want our numbers to start at 0,
    i.e. we want DATEADD(day, 0, min_date::timestamp) to return min_date as the first date in the all_days CTE
    Without the -1, our sequence of numbers would start at 1, leading to a starting date of min_date + 1 in the all_days CTE,
    which would create NULL MRR values for the customer_id(s) with the earliest subscription change events in the dataset
    */
    SELECT (ROW_NUMBER() OVER ()) - 1 AS num
    /*
    We'll use these numbers to generate a sequence of dates to fill in the gaps in our MRR data,
    i.e. to forward-fill MRR values for days where there was no change in MRR
    They are used below in all_days
    The SELECT 1 FROM ... table could be anything, as long as it has enough rows
    to build a sequence of numbers that will cover the full daily history of our oldest customer (1 row per day)
    */
    FROM (SELECT 1 FROM public.stripe_data_pipeline_subscription_item_change_events LIMIT 10000) AS build_sequence_of_numbers
),
all_days AS (
    SELECT DATEADD(day, num, min_date::timestamp) AS generated_date 
    FROM numbers 
    JOIN date_bounds ON 1=1 
    WHERE DATEADD(day, num, min_date::timestamp) <= max_date
),
all_days_all_customers AS (
    /*
    We'll use this to fill in the gaps in our MRR data,
    with a range of dates that is customer-specific, running from the customer's first day up until
    the most recent event day in stripe_data_pipeline_subscription_item_change_events 
    */
    SELECT first_day_by_customer.customer_id,
    all_days.generated_date
    FROM first_day_by_customer
    CROSS JOIN all_days
    WHERE all_days.generated_date >= first_day_by_customer.first_day
),
/*
This is "sparse" because it only has rows for days where there was a change in MRR
We'll use all_days_all_customers to fill in the gaps and then carry the last non-null MRR value forward
*/
cumulative_mrr_sparse AS (
    SELECT mrr_changes_by_day.customer_id,
    mrr_changes_by_day.event_day,
    COALESCE(SUM(mrr_changes_by_day.mrr_change_within_day) OVER (
        PARTITION BY mrr_changes_by_day.customer_id
        ORDER BY mrr_changes_by_day.event_day 
        ROWS BETWEEN UNBOUNDED PRECEDING AND CURRENT ROW
    ), 0) / 100.0 AS mrr 
    FROM mrr_changes_by_day 
    ORDER BY mrr_changes_by_day.customer_id, mrr_changes_by_day.event_day
),
/*
This is "dense" because we have pulled in rows for every day,
but at this point MRR is only populated for days where there was a change in MRR (it will be NULL otherwise)
*/
cumulative_mrr_dense AS (
    SELECT cumulative_mrr_sparse.mrr,
    COALESCE(cumulative_mrr_sparse.event_day, all_days_all_customers.generated_date) as event_day,
    COALESCE(cumulative_mrr_sparse.customer_id, all_days_all_customers.customer_id) as customer_id 
    FROM all_days_all_customers
    LEFT JOIN cumulative_mrr_sparse ON (all_days_all_customers.generated_date = cumulative_mrr_sparse.event_day AND all_days_all_customers.customer_id = cumulative_mrr_sparse.customer_id)
),
/*
This will be used to tag customers whose MRR drops to zero within one day (and who never return)
We'll use this to exclude them from our NRR calculation
For example, see customer_id = 'cus_IQXOHiIVjnMF9W'
Some of these are customers who signed up for the wrong state, or accidentally created a second account,
and we had to cancel and refund them for support reasons
*/
customer_start_and_end_counts AS (
    SELECT subscription_change_events.customer_id,
    SUM(CASE WHEN event_type = 'ACTIVE_START' THEN 1 ELSE 0 END) as n_total_active_start_events,
    SUM(CASE WHEN event_type = 'ACTIVE_START' AND TO_TIMESTAMP(subscription_change_events.event_timestamp, 'YYYY-MM-DD HH24:MI:SS.MS') < first_day_by_customer.first_day + INTERVAL '1 day' THEN 1 ELSE 0 END) as n_early_active_start_events,
    SUM(CASE WHEN event_type = 'ACTIVE_END' AND TO_TIMESTAMP(subscription_change_events.event_timestamp, 'YYYY-MM-DD HH24:MI:SS.MS') < first_day_by_customer.first_day + INTERVAL '1 day' THEN 1 ELSE 0 END) as n_early_active_end_events
    FROM
    public.stripe_data_pipeline_subscription_item_change_events AS subscription_change_events
    JOIN first_day_by_customer ON subscription_change_events.customer_id = first_day_by_customer.customer_id
    GROUP BY subscription_change_events.customer_id
),
month_numbers AS (
    SELECT customer_id,
    event_day,
    /*
    This month_number helper column makes it easy to calculate 12-month NRR
    We want it to be defined only on the last day of the month, so we filter for that in the WHERE clause using LAST_DAY
    */
    ROW_NUMBER() OVER (
        PARTITION BY customer_id
        ORDER BY DATE_TRUNC('month', event_day)
    ) - 1 as month_number
    FROM cumulative_mrr_dense 
    WHERE event_day = LAST_DAY(event_day::date)
),
/*
Helper CTE to identify base plan products and extract plan types using hybrid approach
This uses mysql_products.type = 'base' as the definitive filter for base products,
then applies hybrid plan type extraction logic using mysql_products.name for consistency
*/
base_plan_products AS (
    SELECT 
        mysql_products.stripeproductid as id,
        mysql_products.name,
        -- Hybrid plan type extraction: use mysql_products.plan when available,
        -- otherwise derive from mysql_products.name for consistency
        COALESCE(
            mysql_products.plan,
            CASE
                -- Current active plans (newer naming convention)
                WHEN mysql_products.name LIKE '%Launch Plan%' THEN 'launch'
                WHEN mysql_products.name LIKE '%Grow Plan%' THEN 'grow'
                WHEN mysql_products.name LIKE '%Scale Plan%' THEN 'scale'
                -- Platform plans
                WHEN mysql_products.name LIKE '%Platform Plan%' THEN 'platform'
                -- PAYG (Pay As You Go) plan
                -- Context: https://www.notion.so/usestable/Milestone-2-Pay-As-You-Go-Plan-Experiment-27ee5d85e7518075834cc75e00d3ecaf#27ee5d85e7518075834cc75e00d3ecaf
                WHEN mysql_products.name LIKE '%PAYG%' THEN 'pay_as_you_go'
                -- Historical plans (older naming convention)
                WHEN mysql_products.name LIKE '%Growth Plan%' THEN 'growth'
                WHEN mysql_products.name LIKE '%Startup Plan%' THEN 'startup'
                WHEN mysql_products.name LIKE '%Business Plan%' THEN 'business'
                WHEN mysql_products.name LIKE '%Basic Plan%' THEN 'basic'
                WHEN mysql_products.name LIKE '%Starter Plan%' THEN 'starter'
                -- Custom plans
                WHEN mysql_products.name LIKE '%Custom Plan%' OR mysql_products.name = 'Custom' THEN 'custom'
                -- Custom plan special case: prod_T6rBmN4q4jNucM is "Base + 10,000 mail annual" for cus_LtTx2jp0QLxQ9y
                -- This is a one-off custom base plan that doesn't follow standard naming conventions
                WHEN mysql_products.stripeproductid = 'prod_T6rBmN4q4jNucM' THEN 'custom'
                -- Enterprise plans
                WHEN mysql_products.name LIKE '%Enterprise Plan%' THEN 'enterprise'
                ELSE 'unknown'
            END
        ) AS plan_type
    FROM mysql_products
    WHERE mysql_products.type = 'base'
        AND mysql_products.stripeproductid IS NOT NULL
),
/*
This CTE tracks plan_type changes based on ACTIVE_START and ACTIVE_END events for base plan products
We join with base_plan_products to get plan types using the centralized logic
*/
plan_type_changes_by_day AS (
    SELECT
        DATE_TRUNC('day', TO_TIMESTAMP(subscription_change_events.event_timestamp, 'YYYY-MM-DD HH24:MI:SS.MS')) AS event_day,
        subscription_change_events.customer_id,
        subscription_change_events.event_type,
        subscription_change_events.product_id,
        subscription_change_events.event_timestamp,
        base_plan_products.plan_type
    FROM public.stripe_data_pipeline_subscription_item_change_events AS subscription_change_events
    INNER JOIN base_plan_products ON base_plan_products.id = subscription_change_events.product_id
    WHERE subscription_change_events.event_type IN ('ACTIVE_START', 'ACTIVE_END')
),
/*
This CTE creates a sparse dataset of plan_type by customer by day, only for days when there was a plan change
Similar to cumulative_mrr_sparse, this will be used with forward-fill logic
For days with multiple events, we use the most recent event within the day to determine the final plan_type
*/
plan_type_sparse AS (
    SELECT DISTINCT
        customer_id,
        event_day,
        -- Only consider ACTIVE_START events to avoid NULL plan_type on transition days
        -- This ensures customers transitioning plans on the same day get the new plan_type
        FIRST_VALUE(plan_type) OVER (
            PARTITION BY customer_id, event_day 
            ORDER BY TO_TIMESTAMP(event_timestamp, 'YYYY-MM-DD HH24:MI:SS.MS') DESC
            ROWS BETWEEN UNBOUNDED PRECEDING AND UNBOUNDED FOLLOWING
        ) AS plan_type
    FROM plan_type_changes_by_day
    WHERE event_type = 'ACTIVE_START' -- Only consider ACTIVE_START events
    AND plan_type IS NOT NULL
),
/*
This CTE detects payment cadence changes based on the mysql_prices.recurringinterval field
CRITICAL: Uses price_id (not product_id) to determine the exact billing interval
IMPORTANT: Only considers base products to avoid ambiguity from addons with different cadences

Why this matters:
- Products can have multiple price options (monthly + annual)
- We need to know which specific price the customer was paying for
- Joining on product_id alone would be ambiguous when a product has multiple pricing options
- Customers can have addon products with different payment cadences than their base products
- We only want to track payment cadence changes for base products (mirrors plan_type logic)
- This approach scales robustly as customer payment preferences diversify

Example edge case:
- Customer cus_QMiFOZLQfLdqeO has 2 base products (both semi-annual) and 2 addons (1 annual, 1 monthly)
- Without the base_plan_products filter, FIRST_VALUE could arbitrarily pick any of the 4 cadences
- With the filter, we consistently use the base product cadence (semi-annual)
*/
payment_cadence_changes_by_day AS (
    SELECT DISTINCT
        subscription_change_events.customer_id,
        DATE_TRUNC('day', TO_TIMESTAMP(subscription_change_events.event_timestamp, 'YYYY-MM-DD HH24:MI:SS.MS')) AS event_day,
        subscription_change_events.event_type,
        subscription_change_events.event_timestamp,
        CASE
            WHEN mysql_prices.recurringinterval = 'year' AND mysql_prices.recurringintervalcount = 1 THEN 'year'
            WHEN mysql_prices.recurringinterval = 'month' AND mysql_prices.recurringintervalcount = 1 THEN 'month'
            WHEN mysql_prices.recurringinterval = 'month' AND mysql_prices.recurringintervalcount = 3 THEN 'quarter'
            WHEN mysql_prices.recurringinterval = 'month' AND mysql_prices.recurringintervalcount = 6 THEN 'semi-annual'
            -- Defensive fallback: create obvious 'unknown_X_Y' values for new billing patterns
            -- This ensures Metabase reports will clearly show when new logic is needed
            -- Format: 'unknown_{recurringinterval}_{recurringintervalcount}'
            ELSE 'unknown_' || COALESCE(mysql_prices.recurringinterval, 'null') || '_' || COALESCE(CAST(mysql_prices.recurringintervalcount AS varchar), 'null')
        END as payment_cadence
    FROM public.stripe_data_pipeline_subscription_item_change_events AS subscription_change_events
    -- Only consider base products (mirrors plan_type_changes_by_day logic)
    -- This prevents addon products with different payment cadences from causing ambiguity
    INNER JOIN base_plan_products ON base_plan_products.id = subscription_change_events.product_id
    -- FIXED JOIN STRATEGY: Join on the specific price_id the customer was subscribed to
    -- This ensures we get the actual billing interval for their subscription
    INNER JOIN mysql_prices ON mysql_prices.stripepriceid = subscription_change_events.price_id
    WHERE subscription_change_events.event_type IN ('ACTIVE_START', 'ACTIVE_END')
      AND mysql_prices.recurringinterval IS NOT NULL
),
/*
This CTE creates a sparse dataset of payment_cadence by customer by day, only for days when there was a cadence change
Supports bidirectional transitions (month ↔ year) based on actual subscription billing intervals
*/
payment_cadence_sparse AS (
    SELECT DISTINCT
        customer_id,
        event_day,
        -- Use most recent event within the day to determine final payment_cadence
        -- Only consider ACTIVE_START events to get the cadence of active subscriptions
        FIRST_VALUE(payment_cadence) OVER (
            PARTITION BY customer_id, event_day 
            ORDER BY TO_TIMESTAMP(event_timestamp, 'YYYY-MM-DD HH24:MI:SS.MS') DESC
            ROWS BETWEEN UNBOUNDED PRECEDING AND UNBOUNDED FOLLOWING
        ) AS payment_cadence
    FROM payment_cadence_changes_by_day
    WHERE event_type = 'ACTIVE_START'  -- Only consider active subscriptions
      AND payment_cadence IS NOT NULL
),
/*
Get initial plan type for each customer (plan type on day 0)
This is needed for NRR calculations grouped by plan type - we want to use the STARTING
plan type, not the current plan type, to avoid double-counting customers who upgrade
*/
initial_plan_type_by_customer AS (
    SELECT DISTINCT
        customer_id,
        FIRST_VALUE(plan_type) OVER (
            PARTITION BY customer_id
            ORDER BY event_day
            ROWS BETWEEN UNBOUNDED PRECEDING AND UNBOUNDED FOLLOWING
        ) AS initial_plan_type
    FROM plan_type_sparse
),
/*
Identify customers who switched from grow/scale to custom within 14 days
These are likely sales-led deals where the customer was temporarily put on a standard plan
while the custom contract was being finalized. For NRR purposes, they should be treated
as custom customers from day 0.

Examples found in data:
- cus_OsfpSq4E1j39V4: scale $89 -> custom $649 in 1 day
- cus_NZhyeA5QxjpaZ6: scale $119 -> custom $1999 in 1 day
- cus_P81WnCHsplutyx: scale $89 -> custom $749 in 2 days
*/
early_custom_switch_customers AS (
    SELECT DISTINCT
        plan_type_sparse.customer_id,
        MIN(plan_type_sparse.event_day) OVER (PARTITION BY plan_type_sparse.customer_id) AS first_plan_day,
        -- Find the first day they switched to custom
        MIN(CASE WHEN plan_type_sparse.plan_type = 'custom' THEN plan_type_sparse.event_day END)
            OVER (PARTITION BY plan_type_sparse.customer_id) AS first_custom_day
    FROM plan_type_sparse
    JOIN initial_plan_type_by_customer ipt ON ipt.customer_id = plan_type_sparse.customer_id
    WHERE ipt.initial_plan_type IN ('grow', 'scale')
),
early_custom_switch_adjustment AS (
    SELECT
        customer_id,
        first_plan_day,
        first_custom_day,
        -- Calculate the day_number adjustment: how many days to subtract so that
        -- the first custom day becomes day 0 for NRR purposes
        -- Only apply adjustment if they switched to custom within 14 days
        CASE
            WHEN first_custom_day IS NOT NULL
                 AND DATEDIFF(day, first_plan_day::date, first_custom_day::date) <= 14
            THEN DATEDIFF(day, first_plan_day::date, first_custom_day::date)
            ELSE 0
        END AS day_number_adjustment
    FROM early_custom_switch_customers
),
/*
Count cumulative trial days for each customer as of each event_day
This helps distinguish customers whose first MRR day was truly their first day
versus those who had prior trial usage before converting to paid
*/
cumulative_trial_days AS (
    SELECT
        customer_id,
        day,
        ROW_NUMBER() OVER (
            PARTITION BY customer_id
            ORDER BY day
        ) AS trial_days_count
    FROM (
        -- DISTINCT deduplicates edge cases where a customer has multiple trials on the same day
        -- Example: cus_SoqAedS4pIo2mo had 2 trials start on 2025-09-11:
